focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

By the following evening Elise had chosen delay. Ferrand's voice was already carrying through the schoolroom chalk when she came in with the market basket on her arm, loose and loud and sure of itself, a voice that wanted a wall to hit and a room to take. The paper in her cuff gave under her wrist, gone soft from rain and sweat on the way down through the wine shop cellar.

She had found it where Vautrin liked to leave such things, folded under a chipped saucer near the privy sink, as if an order could pass for refuse if it lay beside enough dirt. The hand was not his. He never wasted his own. But the style was his down to the bones. It asked for names, means, place, before Wednesday night, and warned her not to bring panic instead of fact.

Aboveground the gutters on rue de la Tombe-Issoire were running brown, and the men pasting notices for the May Day marches had already been cursed twice by the police before noon. Belowground the quarry kept its own weather. Damp climbed the walls. Water clicked somewhere deeper in the chalk. The air smelled of lamp oil, wet wool, old stone, and the cabbage soup Clemence had stretched too far at supper.

Inside, she saw at once that the room had already been claimed.

Lucien's crate had been dragged near the wall where he taught the children their letters. Ferrand leaned against it as if it belonged to him, dark moustache damp at the edges, cheap cologne failing to smother the stale sweat in his collar. His satchel rested by his boot. The leather looked decent until one noticed the cracked strap and the buckle repaired with string. Abel stood half a pace from him, too intent to hide it, his narrow coat riding above his wrists and the printer's burns on two fingertips bright as little dead moons when he spread his hand t...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ads below ground had blood on one cuff where his mouth had split and dried there. The wet wool smell had been joined by soap, fear, cheap tobacco, menstrual iron, horse leather from the wagons outside. The state reduced people to paper only after first taking them through the body.

At the bottom of the steps two agents hauled in another woman still arguing. "Those sheets were on account," she shouted. "If they're spoiled you can pay Madame Sorel yourself, you sons of whores."

One of the agents laughed. The other twisted her arm higher until the curse broke open into a cry.

Elise stopped under the archway because if she kept walking too fast she would look guilty and if she stood too still she would become one more piece of furniture in Vautrin's building. Rain had started again, a thin cold spring rain that darkened the courtyard stones and made the horses stamp. A police wagon waited by the gate with its rear door open. Inside, wet hems and boot soles crowded the floorboards.

By evening the corridor was full of damp aprons from more than one yard, split lips, a printer's assistant with blood dried on his cuff, and women trying to remember what the lost wash would cost.

When she turned back toward Montrouge, the city looked unchanged in the miserable way cities often did. Carts kept moving. Men still bought bread. Bells still struck the hour for whoever believed hours belonged equally to everyone. Under her glove the hand that had pushed the report into the drainpipe still smelled faintly of moss and rot, and Vautrin's tidy version of the quarry had begun its own work in her head: printers, wanted men, the rest made legible as conspiracy.

By the time she reached the wine-shop cellar, that smell had mixed with soap, and the question of bombs had followed her below.
